mc.prompt: Which of the following statements is true regarding sufficient statistics?

choices.text: A sufficient statistic provides all information about the parameter of interest contained in the sample.
choices.rationale: This is the definition of a sufficient statistic.

choices.text: A sufficient statistic must always be a function of the sample mean.
choices.rationale: Sufficient statistics can take many forms and are not limited to the sample mean.

choices.text: A sufficient statistic can depend on the parameter of interest.
choices.rationale: By definition, a sufficient statistic does not depend on the parameter.

choices.text: Every statistic derived from a sample is sufficient for the parameter.
choices.rationale: Not every statistic is sufficient; only those that meet the criteria do.

mc.prompt: In the context of the Rao-Blackwell theorem, which of the following statements is correct?

choices.text: Any estimator can be improved by conditioning on the sample mean.
choices.rationale: The improvement is through conditioning on a sufficient statistic, not necessarily the sample mean.

choices.text: The Rao-Blackwell theorem guarantees that the new estimator will have a higher MSE than the original.
choices.rationale: The theorem guarantees that the MSE will be lower or equal, not higher.

choices.text: The new estimator formed by conditioning on a sufficient statistic is guaranteed to be at least as good as the original estimator in terms of MSE.
choices.rationale: This is the essence of the Rao-Blackwell theorem.

choices.text: Only unbiased estimators can be improved using the Rao-Blackwell theorem.
choices.rationale: The theorem applies to any estimator, not just unbiased ones.

mc.prompt: Which of the following distributions has a sufficient statistic that is a function of the maximum of the sample?

choices.text: Exponential(θ)
choices.rationale: For Exponential distributions, the sum of the sample is the sufficient statistic.

choices.text: Normal(μ, σ²)
choices.rationale: For Normal distributions, both the sample mean and sample variance are sufficient.

choices.text: Uniform(0, θ)
choices.rationale: For Uniform distributions, the maximum value is a sufficient statistic.

choices.text: Poisson(θ)
choices.rationale: For Poisson distributions, the sum of the sample is the sufficient statistic.

mc.prompt: According to the factorization criterion, which of the following is a necessary condition for a statistic to be sufficient?

choices.text: The joint distribution must be independent of the parameter.
choices.rationale: The joint distribution must depend on the parameter through the statistic.

choices.text: The joint distribution can be factored into a product of two functions, one of which depends only on the sample.
choices.rationale: This is the essence of the factorization criterion.

choices.text: The statistic must be a linear combination of the sample values.
choices.rationale: Sufficient statistics can take various forms, not just linear combinations.

choices.text: The statistic must be the sample mean.
choices.rationale: The sample mean is not always a sufficient statistic.

mc.prompt: Which of the following is a common pitfall when identifying sufficient statistics?

choices.text: Assuming that all statistics derived from a sample are sufficient.
choices.rationale: Not all statistics are sufficient; only those meeting the criteria are.

choices.text: Confusing the conditions for sufficiency with those for unbiasedness.
choices.rationale: Sufficiency and unbiasedness are distinct concepts.

choices.text: Believing that sufficient statistics must be unique for a given distribution.
choices.rationale: A distribution can have multiple sufficient statistics.

choices.text: Thinking that sufficiency guarantees a lower variance for any estimator.
choices.rationale: Sufficiency does not guarantee lower variance, only that the information about the parameter is captured.

sa.prompt: (a) Let X_1, , X_n be a random sample from a Poisson distribution with parameter B8. (b) Show that T = A3_{i=1}^{n} X_i is a sufficient statistic for B8.
sa.modelAnswer: (a) The likelihood function for a Poisson distribution is given by L(θ | x) = (θ^Σ x_i * e^{-nθ}) / (Π x_i!). To show T is sufficient, we can factor the likelihood function into u(x) and ν(T, θ) as follows: u(x) = 1/Π x_i! and ν(T, θ) = e^{-nθ} * θ^{Σ x_i}. Since the factorization meets the criteria, T is sufficient for θ. (b) Therefore, T = Σ X_i captures all information about θ contained in the sample.
sa.rubric: Correctly identifies the likelihood and shows proper factorization to establish sufficiency.

sa.prompt: (a) Let X_1, , X_n be a random sample from a Uniform(0, θ) distribution. (b) Identify a sufficient statistic for θ and justify your answer using the factorization criterion.
sa.modelAnswer: (a) The likelihood function for a Uniform(0, θ) distribution is L(θ | x) = (1/θ^n) * I(0 < x_i < θ for all i). (b) We can factor this as u(x) = 1 and ν(T, θ) = 1/θ^n where T = max(X_1, ..., X_n). Since u(x) does not depend on θ and ν depends on θ only through T, by the factorization criterion, T is a sufficient statistic for θ.
sa.rubric: Correctly identifies the likelihood and applies the factorization criterion to establish sufficiency.
